title-group.article-title: TICKHUNTER: A Targeted Hybridization-Capture Sequencing Approach for the Detection and Characterization of Tick-borne Pathogens and Blood Meals

abstract.p: Ticks are vectors of many debilitating pathogens that are transmitted during blood meal acquisition. As both vector and pathogen species continue to emerge, there is a critical need for improved pathogen diagnostic and host remnant identification methods to develop interventions. Current pathogen detection methods are limited in their ability to subtype and detect emerging variants and non-vector borne “look-alike” diseases. Determination of tick blood meal sources is also an elusive step for understanding tick interactions with the biotic community in a given environment, with consequences for environmental management and disease prevention strategies. We have created an accessible hybridization capture next-generation sequencing panel called “TICKHUNTER” to improve molecular detection and subtyping of bacterial and parasitic pathogens in both clinical and tick samples. For disease ecology studies, the panel also detects host blood meal sources of interest. An alternative method for unbiased blood meal remnant analysis is also presented. We find that TICKHUNTER is a promising tool for effective and accurate detection of a variety of tick-borne pathogens, based on comparable linearity, limit of detection, sensitivity, and specificity to real-time PCR. Additionally, it can detect and characterize unexpected pathogens due to the large capacity for multiplexing and flexibility in variant detection.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Many important pathogens are transmitted by blood-feeding (hematophagous) arthropod vectors, killing hundreds of thousands of people yearly, infecting millions more, and resulting in billions of USD spent on intervention schemes and medical supplies 1,2. Successful detection of vector-borne pathogens is crucial for mitigating their effects. This requires constant innovation of new diagnostic techniques and adjustments to existing ones. In temperate regions, ticks (Order Ixodida) are the most medically important group of vectors, transmitting a broad variety of viral, eukaryotic, and bacterial pathogens. These include bacteria like Borrelia spp., Anaplasma phagocytophilum, Erhlichia spp., and Rickettsia spp., endoparasites like Babesia microti, and viruses like Powassan virus 3–7. Tick-borne diseases (TBDs) are on the rise globally. In the USA, TBDs account for 77%-95% of the annually reported vector-borne disease cases 8. Ticks frequently carry multiple pathogens simultaneously (co-infections) 9–13, complicating pathogen detection and disease treatment.

Blacklegged ticks (Ixodes scapularis) have complex life cycles, feeding once before molting to the next life stage (or reproducing if adults). Different life stages select hosts of different sizes. In North America, white-tailed deer (Odocoileus virginianus) have been frequently implicated in the growth of tick populations because they are the most frequent host for adult blacklegged ticks, but are considered non-competent for pathogen transmission14. Immature blacklegged ticks can be found on all hosts, including deer 15. These hosts differ in their pathogen competence (i.e., ability of the host to successfully maintain and transmit pathogens to a vector)7,13,17–22,22,23. Because of the difficulty in trapping hosts to determine their association with ticks, the ability to determine the previous bloodmeal of a questing tick has been a fundamental goal of tick research. Linking tick infection status to the bloodmeal host indicates the relative roles of different hosts in the maintenance of vector-borne pathogens and contributes to a broader understanding of vector-host interactions and complex epidemiological transmission networks7,13,24–26.

Hematophagous feeding by arthropod vectors allows for host identification via analysis of DNA contained in blood meals 27,28. Molecular methods have been used for host identification in blood meals due to their high specificity and sensitivity 29. To achieve this, a wide variety of molecular markers for blood meal identification have been described, including repetitive DNA sequences 30, nuclear genes 31, mitochondrial genes 32,33, and ribosomal RNA (rRNA) genes 34. In the case of ticks, amplification of rRNA markers and DNA sequencing have proven to be a specific method for the identification of animal hosts in blood meals 29. However, this method has difficulties detecting DNA in low concentrations in ticks, particularly in nymphs 35, and is not sufficiently sensitive for determining host species 36,37.

Because of the long culture times required for cultivating bacterial tickborne pathogens, molecular testing is the typical modality for direct detection. Parasites such as Babesia spp. cannot be cultured but can be visualized microscopically in blood smears 38. Molecular pathogen detection methods are host-agnostic and therefore also ideal for ecology studies involving multiple hosts. Traditional molecular detection approaches for tick-borne agents rely heavily on polymerase chain reaction (PCR). The PCR technique amplifies pathogen DNA or RNA in a sample to detect target species, typically with a fluorometric analysis. Though the detection specificity and sensitivity for PCR can be very high, the reliance on oligonucleotide primers limits the number of pathogens identified per test run, thus compromising the detection of unexpected agents and only providing partial taxonomic information. PCR can also provide false negative results if the pathogen sequence varies sufficiently from the template used to design the primers.

Hybrid capture next-generation sequencing (HCNGS) is an approach for enriching relevant DNA sequences after nucleic acid extraction and library preparation 39 that overcomes a number of the limitations of PCR. During the hybrid capture process, the target DNA sequences are hybridized to baits based on complementary base pairing. HCNGS can retrieve large genomic fragments with high sequencing coverage, including unknown sequences adjacent to a known template. HCNGS can also accommodate millions of probes, so the number of agents tested is not limited per run. Moreover, the method is compatible with basic molecular biology equipment typically available in hospitals and public health laboratories. Eventually, this may also be extended to field or point-of-care testing. This approach has been successfully applied to research several viral, bacterial, and parasitic agents that cause infectious diseases in humans 40.

In this study, we developed a panel of HCNGS baits and associated protocols for the detection of major bacterial and parasitic tick-borne pathogens (TBP) and host blood meal sources. We named this panel TICKHUNTER. We hypothesized that hybridization capture combined with next-generation sequencing will have comparable diagnostic performance to real-time PCR and allow earlier variant discovery of TBPs, even in cases of coinfection, and successful diagnosis of host blood meal sources. As HCNGS enables the simultaneous detection and sequencing of many genes, it also allows for multilocus sequence typing (MLST) of multiple TBP. We anticipate this methodology facilitating understanding of tick-borne pathogen epidemiology, ecology, and evolution in a number of settings. We expect this panel to enable effective detection of TBPs in under-equipped laboratories, facilitating greater TBD screening. With improved molecular diagnoses of TBPs, potential disease outbreaks will be discerned earlier. Therefore, more prompt and targeted strategies can be made by public health agencies, thus reducing the disease burden and improving human and animal health.

sec.title: Panel design
sec.p: We designed the custom hybridization TICKHUNTER panel with Twist Biosciences (South San Francisco, CA, USA). The purpose of the panel is to detect and sequence tick-borne bacteria and endoparasites from blood, skin biopsies, and tick samples, as well as to characterize the host blood meals from tick samples. A One Health approach was used for target curation, including pathogens of humans, working dogs, pets, and livestock. The panel is intended to target most tick-borne bacteria and endoparasites, either directly or by flexible capture of closely related species (Table 1). In total, the panel targets 43 genes or genomic regions, totaling 16,083 base pairs. The genes or genomic regions targeted for pathogen identification and subtyping were chosen based on highly conserved genes or genomic regions, genes used for speciation and multi-locus typing, and genes related to pathogenicity in certain species/genus 41–49. For A. phagocytophilum and Borrelia spp., seven and nine genes, respectively, were included in the panel to confirm the species detected and perform MLST, enabling association with known pathogenic lineages (Table 1). For host blood meal identification, the panel also includes the sequence of a variable region of the 16S rRNA and conserved flanking regions of eight mammalian species based on the scheme proposed by Karlsson & Holmlund 50; Supplementary Table 1), including human (Homo sapiens), domestic cat (Felis catus), domestic dog (Canis familiaris), house mouse (Mus musculus), white-footed mouse (Peromyscus leucopus), white-tailed deer, big brown bat (Eptesicus fuscus), and raccoon (Procyon lotor). These are a selection of species expected in peri-urban locations in the Northeast US and were selected for the purpose of validation of combined pathogen and host detection. Overall, the panel used for this study, to detect both pathogens and host blood meals, contained 892 overlapping baits of 120 nucleotides in length, covering 30,984 bp in total. It can be ordered from Twist Biosciences using ID: TE-98068339 (June 2024 version).

sec.title: Analytical method validation using synthetic DNA
sec.p: To assess the linearity and limit of detection (LOD) of selected targets of interest in the Northeast US, we used synthetic dsDNA with the known sequences of 8 of the 43 genes or genomic regions included in the panel (Table 1). For each of the eight selected regions, dsDNA of 629 base pairs (bp) in length was synthesized by Integrated DNA Technologies (IDT, Newark, NJ, USA). The eight genes target eight different tick-borne pathogens, including Rickettsia spp. (or Panrickettsia), Rickettsia rickettsii, Borrelia burgdorferi, Borrelia miyamotoi, Ehrlichia chaffeensis, Anaplasma marginale, A. phagocytophilum, and Ba. microti. A list of the sequences used for this assay is in Supplementary Table 2. The synthesized dsDNA was pooled together at an initial concentration of 105 DNA copies/ μL each. To assess the linearity of the panel, 10-fold serial dilutions from 105 to 10−1 copies/μL were prepared from the pooled synthetic dsDNA containing the eight selected genes. Each dilution series from 105 to 10−1 copies/μL was performed with seven repetitions. For the LOD assay, 2-fold serial dilutions from 5 to 0.078125 DNA copies/μL of the eight pooled genes were performed five separate times.

To better approximate the complexity of clinical samples, an LOD experiment was then performed with a commercial quantitative genomic DNA standard for Ba. microti (ATCC PRA-398DQ) spiked into blood and ear-notch samples from specific-pathogen-free laboratory mice (FVB/NJ, JAX #001800) that were being euthanized for an unrelated study approved by the Cornell University Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee (IACUC, Protocol 2007–0115). The concentrations of Ba. microti dilutions (both synthetic dsDNA and genomic) were confirmed by droplet digital PCR (ddPCR) using the ddPCR Supermix for Probes (Bio-Rad, Hercules, CA, USA), and the primers Babs_syn_F (5’- CGGGTGCACTATCCGATTTAT-3’), Babs_syn_R (5’-GCACTGGCCTCATCATCTT-3’) and probe Babs_syn_P (5’-FAM-TCACCGACGATGTATCCGAGAAGGT-BHQ-3’). ddPCR was carried out using the standard method for probes using the Droplet Generator Oil for Probes (Biorad), the QX200 Droplet Generator (Biorad), and the QX200 Droplet Reader (Biorad). After the ddPCR, the DNA concentration (copies/μL) for each sample was calculated using the QX Manager software (Biorad). The final DNA concentration in both sample types ranged from 5 to 0.078125 DNA copies/μL. DNA extraction in blood and ear notches for white-footed mice was carried out using the Quick-DNA/RNA Pathogens MagBead Kit (Zymo Research). For ear notches, the initial volume for the DNA extraction was 500 μL, with 50 μL of quantified Ba. microti DNA added into 450 μL of DNA/RNA Shield (Zymo Research) containing an ear notch of approximately 5 mm in diameter. Zirconia/Silica beads of 2.0mm were used to homogenize the ear notches in the BeadBlaster D2400 (Benchmark Scientific) microtube homogenizer at 6.0 speed for two 45-second cycles, with 30 seconds of intermission. After homogenization, samples were centrifuged at 14,000 rpm for 1 minute, followed by a one-hour incubation with 25 μL of Proteinase K at room temperature. After incubation, samples were centrifuged for 3 minutes at 14,000 rpm, and 300 μL of the supernatant was used for the DNA/RNA extraction, which was carried out on the KingFisher Apex (Thermo Fischer Scientific), following the suggested standard method.

sec.title: Field sampling
sec.p: To test the diagnostic performance of the TICKHUNTER panel for pathogen detection in surrogate clinical samples, blood and skin biopsy (ear notch) samples were collected from white-footed mice and raccoons in Queens and Staten Island, two boroughs of New York City (NYC) known to have low (Queens) and high (Staten Island) tick-borne pathogen circulation 13, with multiple vector species established. Field sampling was approved by the IACUC of Columbia University (AC-AABM9557 and AC-AABS3611), Cornell University (2022–0198), and the US Army Animal Care and Use Review Office (ACURO #TB210019.e001). A License to Collect was also obtained from the New York State Department of Environmental Conservation (License # 2704).

White-footed mice and raccoons were live-trapped and sampled from the beginning of August - early September 2023 as this time period corresponds with when larval I. scapularis, H. longicornis and A. americanum ticks actively search for a host blood meal in NYC 51. For all individual white-footed mice and raccoons captured, all parasitizing ticks were collected, a small (2mm) ear tissue biopsy was taken, blood was collected, and all animals were tagged using metal ear tags before being released back to the trapping location. Tick and tissue samples were placed into prefilled vials with RNA/DNA Shield (Zymo Research, Irvine, CA) and stored at 4°C until further processing. White-footed mouse blood was collected and stored in EDTA microtubes at −80°C and raccoon blood was collected and stored in DNA/RNA Shield blood collection tubes and stored at 4°C until further processing. All ticks collected from white-footed mice and raccoons were identified to species and life stage using microscopy 52,53.

sec.title: Nucleic acid extraction from field samples
sec.p: Samples processed included 64 ear notches and 39 blood samples from white-footed mice, and 24 blood samples from raccoons. DNA and RNA were co-extracted from ear notch tissue samples using the Quick-DNA/RNA Pathogens MagBead Kit (Zymo Research) as mentioned above for the ear notches of control mouse samplesDNA from mouse blood was extracted using the Monarch Genomic DNA Purification kit (New England Biolabs, NEB), using the standard protocol for whole blood extraction. DNA and RNA from raccoon blood samples were extracted using the MagMAX CORE Nucleic Acid Purification Kit (Applied Biosystems) following the whole blood workflow provided by the manufacturer. Prior to extraction, blood samples were equilibrated to room temperature for one hour. The extraction process was performed on the KingFisher Apex (Thermo Fischer Scientific), following the suggested standard method. The DNA extracted from the blood and ear notch samples was used for real-time PCR and library preparation, followed by hybridization capture and sequencing.

To test the TICKHUNTER panel for targeted host blood meal analysis, 8 I. scapularis host-attached larval tick pools were analyzed, which had been removed from 8 individual free-ranging white-footed mice. Additionally, a collection of questing ticks collected from different environments were examined to compare the targeted hybrid capture method against the untargeted approach described in section 2.10. Hard ticks from previously published studies included 7 adult I. scapularis from Long Island and the Hudson Valley (NY, USA) 54,55. Soft questing ticks included 7 Ornithodoros hermsi (4 adults and 3 nymphs) from Washington and Oregon states (Supplementary Table 3). An additional I. scapularis nymph and D. variabilis adult, both removed from pet dogs, were used for primer titration. DNA extracted from one remnant cow lung diagnostic sample and two ticks collected from ponies were used as process controls. Hard ticks were individually placed in microtubes with 400 μL of 1X phosphate buffered saline (PBS) and a single hollow brass bead and homogenized on a Mini-Beadbeater-96 (BioSpec Products, Inc., Bartlesville, OK) for 5 minutes at 2,100 rpm. Tick lysates were then centrifuged at 2,500 rpm for 5 minutes. DNA and RNA were extracted from ticks using the Quick-DNA/RNA Pathogen MagBead (Zymo Research) on the KingFisher Apex (Thermo Fischer Scientific),

All nucleic acid extractions included a negative control, and final DNA concentration was measured on the Qubit Flex Fluorometer (Invitrogen) using the Qubit 1X dsDNA High Sensitivity or Broad Range assay kits (Invitrogen). Resulting aliquots from all samples were stored at −80 °C until further use.

sec.title: Real-time PCR for pathogen detection
sec.p: The DNA extracted from raccoon and white-footed mouse samples collected in NYC were used to perform gold standard real time (quantitative) PCR to detect Bo. burgdorferi, Ba. microti, A. phagocytophilum, and Bartonella spp. 45,56–58. The real time PCR was performed on the Applied Biosystems QuantStudio 3 Real-Time PCR System (Applied Biosystems, USA). Each reaction contained TaqMan™ Fast Virus 1-Step Multiplex Master Mix (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA), forward and reverse primers listed in Table 2 at 10 pM fluorescent dye labelled probe (Bo. burgdorferi, A. phagocytophilum, and Bartonella spp. at 10 pM, and Ba. microti at 20 pM), and DNA template (4 μL). Nuclease-free water was added to a final volume of 20 μL. Reactions were performed using the following thermocycling conditions: 50°C for 2 min, 95°C for 10 min, followed by 45 cycles of 95°C for 15 s and 60°C for 1 min.

sec.title: Library preparation and hybridization capture
sec.p: DNA libraries were prepared from the dilutions of the pooled synthetic dsDNA and the extracted DNA from mouse blood and ear notches in which genomic DNA from Ba. microti was spiked in. For libraries, the amount of DNA added varied depending on the concentration to achieve a mass of 50ng in a volume of 40 μL. To test the sensitivity and specificity of the panel in field-collected samples, libraries were prepared from the DNA extracted from blood, ear notches, and ticks. All libraries were prepared using the Twist Library Preparation Enzymatic Fragmentation Kit 2.0 (Twist Biosciences) with the Illumina TruSeq-compatible Twist CD Index Adapter Set 1–96 (Twist Biosciences), following the standard protocol aiming for an average library size of 300bp. The final DNA concentration of the libraries was quantified using the Qubit Flex Fluorometer (Invitrogen) and the Qubit 1X dsDNA High Sensitivity or Broad Range assay kits (Invitrogen). These libraries were used for hybridization capture using the TICKHUNTER panel (Twist design ID: TE-98068339) and the Twist Universal Blockers and Hybridization Reagents (Twist Biosciences). The Twist Target Enrichment Standard Hybridization v2 Protocol (Twist Biosciences) for multiplexing eight indexed libraries was used, with a hybridization incubation of 15 to 17 hours. The resulting hybridized libraries were sequenced on the MiSeq (Illumina) using the MiSeq Reagent Kit V3, reading length 215 bp (Illumina). See Figure 1 for a graphical overview of the hybridization capture method, which includes three main parts: library preparation, capture-on-beads preparation, and hybridization. Protocols for preparing DNA libraries and hybridized enriched libraries, along with key reagents, are listed in Table 3.

To determine if it was possible to obtain results using the portable iSeq platform (Illumina), the libraries from the samples that tested positive for Bo. burgdorferi and were previously sequenced using the MiSeq platform, were also sequenced using the iSeq platform. For sequencing on the iSeq, an iSeq100 V2 cartridge and flow cell were used, with a read length of 151 bp. For each result, the number of genes from the MLST that were sequenced, as well as the percentage coverage, were recorded.

sec.title: Analytical method validation of the TICKHUNTER panel
sec.p: We estimated the linearity and LOD of the panel with the reads resulting from hybridization capture of the diluted pooled synthetic dsDNA and the genomic DNA of Ba. microti spiked in mouse blood and ear notches. For this purpose, the reads were directly transferred from Illumina Basespace to the Chan Zuckerberg ID (CZID, https://czid.org) metagenomics platform. This free, user-friendly platform enables the easy execution of bioinformatic pipelines to analyze sequences in a dataset 59,60. For this study, we used the metagenomics pipeline specifically designed to identify microorganisms within a mixed sequence dataset. We used the total number of reads across all assembled contigs (contig r) for each of the eight genes for each dilution of the synthetic dsDNA. For the linearity assay, we used a linear regression to estimate the linear relationship between the average computed contig r values for the five replicates of each dilution and the known DNA concentration. The LOD of each gene was calculated by the fraction of positive samples (i.e. those in which at least one contig r was obtained) for each dilution used across the five replicates. The measured fractions were fitted to a sigmoidal curve in R, and the LOD was estimated as the minimum DNA concentration in which a positive result was found in at least 95% of the replicates 61. We generated an R script that models the relationship between pathogen concentration (log-transformed) and the fraction of positive samples using a sigmoidal function. It fits the model with robust optimization via the nlsLM function, estimating parameters a (slope) and b (midpoint). The script calculates the limit of detection as the concentration where the fraction positive reaches 0.95 (y=0.95) by solving the inverted sigmoid function. It visualizes the data with a fitted curve, marking the LOD with dashed lines, and converts the LOD back to the normal scale for interpretation. The results of the hybridization capture from the blood and ear notches of raccoons and white-footed mice were used for the sensitivity and specificity assays.

The results of the hybridization capture experiments were also downloaded as FASTQ file pairs and uploaded as combined pairs to the public health instance of the free Galaxy informatics platform, https://galaxytrakr.org/ 62. Trimmomatic 63 v.0.39 was used to filter poor quality reads and trim poor-quality bases. Once the paired sequence data was generated, BWA-MEM2 64 v.2.2.1 was used to map the trimmed reads obtained after the hybrid capture to the reference pathogen sequences included in the panel (Table 1, also available in .fasta format on GitLab). Then, the generated .bam files from BWA-MEM2 were uploaded to Geneious Prime 2023.2.1 (Dotmatics, Bishop’s Stortford, UK) for manual checking. For sensitivity and specificity testing, three factors were considered when deciding if a result is a true positive or false positive, including the number of reads, contig coverage, and mapping quality. First, the results of the hybridization capture were computed as negative if no reads were detected. Secondly, the matching results of the hybridization capture were computed as positive if the matching area covers more than 70% of the reference sequences ncluded in the panel. The Geneious mapping quality tool was used to determine confidence that the read has been mapped to the correct location (https://assets.geneious.com/manual/2021.1/static/GeneiousManualse50.html). The results were computed as negative if the mapping quality was lower than 50. These results were then compared to those of the respective real-time PCRs.

sec.title: MLST, phylogenetic analyses, and sequencing platform comparison
sec.p: For A. phagocytophilum and Bo. burgdorferi, the complete or partial sequences obtained from the genes targeted for MLST were uploaded to the A. phagocytophilum database 46 or the Bo. burgorferi database 65 of the Public Databases for Molecular Typing and Microbial Genome Diversity (PubMLST 66), respectively. Each A. phagocytophilum and Bo. burgdorferi detected was typed following their respective MLST scheme 46 to generate an allelic profile. This profile was used to find the allelic groups (also known as sequence types or STs) that closely match those of the samples from this study. The partial or complete sequences of the seven genes of each A. phagocytophilum obtained in this study were concatenated and aligned with those of the concatenated sequences of the closely matched STs using the Clustal Omega algorithm 67 in Geneious. This alignment was used to construct a maximum likelihood (ML) phylogenetic tree in MEGA11 68.

For the samples in which the sequence of the 23S rRNA of Babesia spp. and the ssrA gene of Bartonella spp. were obtained, a BLAST search was performed to find closely related sequences. For Bo. burgorferi, the three complete sequences of the ospC gene obtained in this study were aligned with the sequences of the 33 major groups (MG) of Bo. burgdorferi sensu stricto 69. For these three pathogens, the sequences were aligned, and an ML phylogenetic tree was constructed in the same manner as for sequences of A. phagocytophilum.

sec.title: Reference-based host blood meal analysis from the hybridization capture method
sec.p:
The results of HCNGS were used to identify the blood meal remnant sources of ticks using a limited panel of baits. The variable region of each of the sequences included in the panel (~169-nucleotide in length, Supplementary Table 1) was extracted to use as the reference for the mapping of the obtained reads. A custom Galaxy workflow was built to filter reads with fastp, map to reference with BWA-MEM2, and quantify reads that mapped to each host with Samtools. A link to the workflow can be found here: https://usegalaxy.eu/u/jdz34/w/tick-map-imported-from-url. Sequencing coverage depth was calculated using the following formula:
CoverageDepth=(NumberofReads×ReadLength)÷NumberofGenesΣGeneLengths

sec.title: Host blood meal using the untargeted 16S rRNA-based PCR method
sec.p: Host blood meal DNA was amplified using primers targeting the 16S rRNA gene (UnivMamF: 5’- GACGAGAAGACCCTATGGAGC-3’, UnivMamR: 5’- TCCGAGGTCGCCCCAACC-3), described by Karlsson & Holmlund50, and first applied to NGS by Tillmar et al.70. Different combinations of forward and reverse primer concentrations were tested to optimize DNA yield during PCR amplification 71. A matrix of primer concentrations ranging from 100 to 1000 nM and DNA extracted from two dog-removed ticks were used as templates during the experiment. Optimal results were obtained with the highest concentration for both forward and reverse primers. PCR amplification was performed in 50 μL reactions, using the following cycling conditions: 94 °C for 2 minutes; 35 cycles of denature 94 °C for 15 seconds, anneal 55°C for 15 seconds, extend 68 °C for 30 seconds; final extension 72°C for 10 minutes. Those samples with a band of 98–120 bp (visualized on a 2% agarose gel) were moved forward for sequencing. Purification was performed with Ampure XP beads (Beckman Coulter Life Sciences) with a 1.85X bead to sample ratio, followed by an abbreviated Illumina DNA Prep library preparation, replacing the final cleanup with the Ampure XP cleanup as before. Cleaned, barcoded amplicons were adjusted to 4 nM, pooled, and sequenced with 2x100 bp Illumina chemistry. The MiSeq run targeted half of million reads per sample, generated 8,466,007 paired-end reads which were analyzed for consensus. Analysis of sequences was performed using QIIME2 72. First, sequences were denoised into Amplicon Sequence Variants (ASVs) using ‘dada2 denoised-paired’ plugin with trimming options set to 6, truncating options to 70. Taxonomy predictions were assigned to the ASVs with plugin ‘feature-classifier classify-sklearn’ using a homemade classifier from reference mitochondrial sequences. A reference classifier was adapted to our primer set using plugin ‘feature-classifier extract-reads’ options ‘--p-f-primer GACGAGAAGACCCTATGGAGC --p-r-primer TCCGAGGTCGCCCCAACC --p-min-length 70 --p-max-length 150’. Then those trimmed sequences were trained into a classifier using plugin ‘feature-classifier fit-classifier-naïve-bayes’. The final ASV matrix was exported using plugin ‘tools export’ (ASV table in biom format) followed by ‘biom convert’ from biom-format v2.1.0 73 to create a tsv file. Taxonomy was downloaded from online view (qiime2 view) of qzv taxonomy file made with ‘metadata tabulate’ plugin. ASV tsv file and taxonomy tsv file downloaded were combined and investigated manually to detect Protein sources.

sec.title: Targeted host retrotransposon-based real-time PCR assay for blood meal identification
sec.p: For comparison purposes, a retrotransposon-based quantitative PCR assay was also employed on selected tick samples 74. This approach utilized species-specific primers and probes to target and amplify host retrotransposons, highly replicated genetic elements that may remain after tick blood meal digestion and molting. The real-time PCR assay consisted of three multiplexed sets of primers and probes, each specific to a different species’ short interspersed repetitive elements 74. Multiplex 1 contained primers and probes targeting mouse (Peromyscus), rabbit (Lagomorpha), and vole (Arvicolinae) retrotransposons. Multiplex 2 contained primers and probes targeting shrew (Soricidae), deer (Odocoileus), and skunk/raccoon (Mephitidae/Procyon) retrotransposons. Multiplex 3 contained primers and probes targeting bird, squirrel (Sciuridae), and opossum (Didelphidae) retrotransposons. Following the protocol developed by Goethert (2021), each selected tick sample was tested with each multiplex in duplicate, on the CFX Opus 96 Real-Time PCR System (Bio-Rad).

sec.title: Linearity and LOD of the TICKHUNTER panel
sec.p: The linearity of the TICKHUNTER panel was evaluated using 10-fold serial dilutions (ranging from 105 to 10−1 copies/μL) of pooled synthetic dsDNA containing the partial sequence of 8 genes included in the panel (Table 1). These eight genes were detected and sequenced simultaneously in a single hybridization capture reaction. The linear relationship between the average computed contig r values obtained after hybridization capture for the five replicates of each dilution for the eight genes and the known DNA concentration (copies/μL) is shown in Figure 2. The panel demonstrated a strong linear relationship between the average contig r and the known number of DNA copies/μL for each of the targeted genes, with coefficient of determination (R2) values ranging from 0.97 to 0.98. The narrow standard errors around the regression lines further validated the precision of the assay in detecting pathogen DNA at decreasing concentration values (Table 4).

The LOD of the TICKHUNTER panel was evaluated using 2-fold serial dilutions of pooled synthetic dsDNA containing the sequence of 8 genes included in the panel (Supplementary Table 2). Table 5 summarizes the minimum detectable DNA copy numbers achieved using the TICKHUNTER panel followed by hybridization capture and sequencing. The LOD is expressed as DNA copies/μL and their corresponding total DNA copies, calculated based on the initial sample volume added for each assay. Among the pathogens tested, the detection of A. marginale using the msp1b gene exhibited the lowest detection threshold, with 0.09 DNA copies/μL, equating to 1.9 total DNA copies. Similarly, the Panrickettsia detection using the OmpA gene demonstrated a low detection limit of 0.21 DNA copies/μL (4.2 total DNA copies). The LOD for the detection of Bo. miyamotoi using the ITS was 2.51 DNA copies/μL, corresponding to 50.2 total DNA copies, representing the highest detection threshold in this study. The detection of other pathogens, including R. rickettsii (using the RRi6 gene) and A. phagocytophilum (using the msp2-B/msp-2C genes), demonstrated intermediate detection limits of 0.84 DNA copies/μL (16.8 total DNA copies) and 1.49 DNA copies/μL (29.9 total DNA copies), respectively. Supplementary Figure 1 shows the fitted sigmoidal curves for the estimation of the LOD for each gene.

The LOD of the TICKHUNTER panel in clinical samples was evaluated by spiking-in known concentrations of genomic DNA of Ba. microti in whole blood and ear notches of laboratory mice (Table 6). The LOD for whole blood was 1.85 DNA copies/μL, corresponding to a total of 92 DNA copies. In ear notch samples, the LOD was higher, with 3.04 DNA copies/μL, equating to a total of 152 DNA copies. These results indicate that the panel is sensitive enough to detect Ba. microti in both blood and tissue samples, with slightly lower detection thresholds in blood compared to ear notches. Supplementary Figure 2 shows the fitted sigmoidal curves for the estimation of the LOD for each sample type.

sec.title: Diagnostic sensitivity and specificity of TICKHUNTER
sec.p: The sensitivity and specificity of the TICKHUNTER panel was estimated using 64 samples of ear-notches from white footed mice collected in NYC. We chose Borrelia and Bartonella for this analysis, representing one vector-borne and one “look-alike” pathogen, because they had the most positives in the samples. For Borrelia, the panel demonstrated a sensitivity of 94.7% and a specificity of 91.1% (Table 7). According to the two-by-two contingency table (Table 8), the panel correctly identified 18 true positives and 41 true negatives out of 64 samples, with 4 false negatives and 1 false positive. For Bartonella, the sensitivity and specificity were 95.8% and 88.6%, respectively (Table 7). The corresponding two-by-two contingency table (Table 9) showed that the panel correctly identified 23 true positives and 70 true negatives, with 9 false negatives and 1 false positive.

sec.title: Overall Detection Results from ear notches and whole blood
sec.p: Out of 64 white-footed mice ear notch samples, by real-time PCR, one sample tested positive for A. phagocytophilum, 22 samples tested positive for Bo. burgdorferi, 11 for Ba. microti, and 7 for Bartonella spp. In comparison, by HCNGS, we detected 20 Bo. burgdorferi-positive samples, 7 Babesia-positive samples, and 1 Bartonella-positive sample, with no detections of A. phagocytophilum. Additionally, 11 white-footed mouse ear notch samples tested positive for Rickettsia spp. by hybridization capture method.

Out of 39 white-footed mouse blood samples, one tested positive for A. phagocytophilum, 3 for Bo. burgdorferi, 6 for Ba. microti using real-time PCR methods. With HCNGS, one sample tested positive for A. phagocytophilum, 2 for Bo. burgdorferi, 7 for Babesia spp., and 27 for Bartonella spp. Additionally, Rickettsia spp. was detected in one blood sample using the HCNGS method. For raccoon blood samples, 22 out of 24 tested positive for Babesia spp.
Detailed information for animal samples is included in Supplementary Table 4 – 6. The full pathogen detection results are included in Supplementary Tables 7 and 8.

sec.title: Pathogen typing from blood and ear notch samples
sec.p: From the 24 samples of raccoon blood processed, a segment of the 23S rRNA of Babesia spp. was detected and sequenced from 22 samples. The sa1 gene of Ba. microti was not detected in any sample, either by real-time PCR or HCNGS. The obtained 23S rRNA sequences shared 99.98% similarity with each other and clustered together in a single group (Figure 3). This group was closely related to three Babesia microti MGB JLBM strains (1D0, 1D7, and 1D11), identified from human cases in New York State, USA, in 2022 (Figure 3). Genetic comparison of these 3 Ba. microti with the 22 sequences obtained in this study, showed that they are 98.2% similar.

For the blood and ear notch samples from three raccoons (RA516, RA517, RA593), we obtained complete or partial sequences of the seven genes targeted for the MLST of A. phagocytophilum (Supplementary Table 9). Fewer genes were detected and sequenced from ear notch samples, with only one to three genes sequenced, compared to blood samples where a minimum of six genes were recovered (Supplementary Table 9). Based on the more comprehensive results from blood, the closest allelic group (or ST) for the samples detected from two raccoons (RA516, RA517) was ST64, and for the third raccoon (RA593) was ST161. Since it was not possible to obtain the complete sequence of the seven genes included in the MLST scheme for all the samples, we concatenated the sequences of five genes (dnaN, glyA, mdh, pheS, sucA) from two samples (RA516, RA593). These were the longest overlapping sequences obtained from these two samples, allowing for a more complete genetic comparison with other sequences. A phylogenetic tree using these concatenated sequences (915nt) and those of the closest allelic groups showed that the two A. phagocytophilum detected in this study are in a group with three other allelic groups (ST64, ST161, ST215), all of which have been reported in the blood of humans or horses with granulocytic anaplasmosis in the USA between 1969 and 2007 (Figure 4). An additional A. phagocytophilum detected to date in the US in an I. scapularis tick but not in humans (Ap-V1) does not group with the sequences obtained in this study or those from human cases in the USA (Figure 4).

We also obtained partial or complete sequences of the other three targeted genes of A. phagocytophilum (msp4, msp2-B, msp-2C) from the same three raccoons (Supplementary Table 10). For two of the sequences (one of msp-2C and one of msp2-B), we sequenced the complete gene, plus flanking sequences (Supplementary Table 10). As with the MLST genes, longer and more complete sequences were detected in whole blood than in ear notch samples (Supplementary Table 10). However, it was possible to obtain sequences, complete or partial, for all the samples, facilitating the sequence comparison between sample types. The partial sequences of the msp2-B (465 nt) from the same individual were identical, while a comparison between individuals showed that the sequences were 99.8% similar. A phylogenetic tree of this partial region of the msp2-B gene for both whole blood and ear notch samples for the three raccoons revealed their grouping with those from the allelic group ST64 (Figure 5), as seen using the MLST genes (Figure 4).

From 64 ear notch samples from white-footed mice, 18 were positive for Bo. burgdorferi using both the real-time PCR and the HCNGS methods. From the 9 genes targeted for the MLST of Bo. burgdorferi, a minimum of four and a maximum of seven genes were sequenced in the positive samples (Table 10). It was not possible to detect and sequence the pepX and pyrG genes in any of the samples. Using the MiSeq sequencing platform, it was possible to detect and sequence the 7 genes even in samples with high ct values (i.e. 35) in the real-time PCR. In contrast, using the iSeq sequencing platform, not all the seven genes were sequenced in samples with higher ct values. Using both platforms, the percentage of coverage decreased as the ct value increased, although it was possible to obtain 100% coverage in certain genes using the MiSeq platform (Table 10).

Out of the 18 samples that tested positive for Bo. burgdorferi, we successfully obtained the complete OspC gene for three of them. A phylogenetic tree constructed from these sequences, along with those from 33 major groups (MG) of Bo. burgdorferi sensu stricto, indicates that the three sequences belong to MG A (Figure 6).

From the 39 samples of blood from white-footed mice processed, the sequence of the ssrA gene of Bartonella spp. was detected and sequenced in 27 samples. A comparison of a 166 bp region of the gene obtained from all the sequences revealed that they were 96.9% similar. A phylogenetic tree of a longer region (242 bp) using 17 of the positive samples shows that the sequences obtained in this study fall into two separate groups (Figure 7), one within a big group together with Bar. vinsonii, Bar. grahamii, Bar. japonica and Bar. coopersplainsensis (Group 1), and another closely related to Bar. rochalimae (Group 2). The sequences from Group 1 are 99.8% similar, and those from Group 2 are identical. Each group contains sequences obtained from mice collected in both Queens and Staten Island.

sec.title: Host-attached larvae.
sec.p: Eight pools of larvae removed from eight free-ranging white-footed mice were used as proof of concept to assess the simultaneous detection of host blood meal and tick-borne pathogens using the TICKHUNTER panel. The correct host was identified (i.e. white-footed mouse), from the eight pools of larvae removed from the same animal (Table 11). Simultaneous detection of 1–3 pathogens from the larvae (Table 11) in addition to the Rickettsia endosymbiont of I. scapularis was also demonstrated. Pathogenic gene sequence coverage was as high as ~2,000X from a single larva and ~53,000X from 2 pooled larvae (Table 11). Additional metadata for these larvae are included in Supplementary Table 11. The calculation details for average coverage depths are included in Supplementary Table 12.

sec.title: Questing adult hard ticks.
sec.p: Blood meal remnant composition was characterized for eight I. scapularis adult ticks through two different PCR-based approaches: i) host-agnostic conventional amplification of the mitochondrial 16S rRNA gene followed by sequencing; and ii) targeted real-time PCR for retrotransposons associated with specific hosts 74. The ticks were selected from a previous study based on their infection with Powassan virus lineage II 54,55. For the first approach, all ticks had successful conventional PCR amplification and were subsequently sequenced on MiSeq chemistry (Table 12). After filtering, denoising, and merging of input sequences and chimeric sequence detection, 271 features (i.e. unique DNA sequences) with a total frequency of 7,041,946 were annotated. Nucleotide identity of features averaged 98.7% (range: 77.8% - 100%) with reference mitochondrial genomes from NCBI. Features were grouped in different taxonomical levels, namely Phylum Chordata, Families Fringillidae and Felidae; Genera Canis, Felis, and Odocoileus; and species House finch (Haemorhous mexicanus), domestic dog (Canis lupus familiaris), cat (Felis catus), cow (Bos taurus), and human (Homo sapiens) (Supplementary Figure 3). Unassigned features remained outside the expected amplicon size and were present in all samples. Details about the proportion of features observed in each sample are indicated in Supplementary Table 3. The attempt to detect host retrotransposons by real-time PCR was unsuccessful (all samples were negative for mouse, rabbit, vole, shrew, deer, skunk/raccoon, bird, squirrel, and opossum, with each sample tested in duplicate).

sec.title: Questing adult and nymphal soft ticks
sec.p: Soft ticks were used as a proof of principle for identifying bloodmeal sources to guide serosurveillance in areas with unidentified human illnesses. On soft ticks, we compared: i) targeted host detection with HCNGS (using the TICKHUNTER panel); and ii) host-agnostic mitochondrial PCR metabarcoding as in 3.6.2 (Table 13). The hybrid-capture approach yielded some sequences that were from closely related species (e.g. Peromyscus leucopus versus P. maniculatus). If there were no closely related species on the panel (e.g. Myotis californicus) then the hybridization capture failed. The host-agnostic approach provided additional resolution on the nymphs (e.g. Low’s squirrel in ESV-4–3), although it also appears to have detected environmental contamination from animal-source foods (e.g. pig, fish, chicken) and humans.

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: In this study we have provided evidence that a new hybrid-capture panel, TICKHUNTER, is an effective new molecular tool for identifying and subtyping tick-borne pathogens together with those causing “look-alike” diseases, as well as blood meal sources. Limit of detection, sensitivity, and specificity results suggest that the TICKHUNTER panel is as effective or better than current molecular techniques. The evaluation of the pilot panels’ performance was mainly conducted focusing on 8 bacterial pathogens considering their public health significance to the US. Linear regression analysis showed that the resulting bacterial copy number after HCNGS using the TICKHUNTER panel had a good linear relationship (R2 ≥ 0.97) for all the eight genes tested in solution (Figure 2). According to the LOD results, using the TICKHUNTER panel it is possible to detect and sequence eight different bacterial and parasite genes simultaneously, suggesting that this panel has the potential to be used to detect multiple tick-borne pathogens in samples at low concentrations. We compared the LOD results of our designed method with LOD results reported by the US Centers for Disease Control using real-time PCR 56. For example, the detection limit of A. phagocytophilum was 20 DNA copies when using real-time PCR, while our results showed a detection limit of 29.9 DNA copies when using HCNGS (Table 5). We were also able to detect pathogens including Bo. burgdorferi, Bo. miyamotoi, and Ba. microti with low DNA copies. Specifically, the lowest detectable DNA concentrations ranged from 0.09 copies/μL for A. marginale (msp1b) to 2.51 copies/μL for Bo. miyamotoi (ITS). Total DNA copies detected varied from 1.9 copies for A. marginale to 50.2 copies for Bo. miyamotoi. Additionally, we detected Rickettsia rickettsii (0.84 copies/μL, 16.8 total copies), Panrickettsia (0.21 copies/μL, 4.2 total copies), Ba. microti (1.14 copies/μL, 22.8 total copies), Ehrlichia chaffeensis (1.14 copies/μL, 22.8 total copies), and A. phagocytophilum (1.49 copies/μL, 29.9 total copies). These results highlight HCNGS’s capability to accurately identify pathogens across a wide range of DNA concentrations, making it an effective tool for sensitive pathogen surveillance.

Ba. microti is the most significant tick-borne intraerythrocytic protozoan causing human babesiosis in the United States 75. Additionally, a higher diversity of Babesia spp. and related piroplasms has been reported in wildlife and certain human populations in specific regions 10,76–79. The Ba. microti surface antigen 1 is a secreted protein critical for the parasite’s invasion of host red blood cells, making the sa1 gene a typical key target for detection 80. From 24 raccoon blood samples analyzed, a segment of the 23S rRNA gene from Babesia spp. was detected and sequenced in 22 samples. However, the sa1 gene specific to Ba. microti was not found, indicating the pathogen is not Ba. microti. The obtained sequences showed remarkable similarity (99.98%) and clustered with and were 98.2% similar to three Ba. microti sequences from human cases in New York State in 2022 (Figure 3). These findings suggest the presence of a Ba.microti-like species in raccoons, that is phylogenetically related to but grouped separately from Ba. microti. Babesia microti-like species were reported by Garrett et al. 2019 81 in raccoons from the southeastern US, including Georgia, Florida, Missouri, Texas, California, and West Virginia. The results highlight the ability of the HCNGS detection method to identify unexpected but potentially significant taxa that are not identical to the baits, demonstrating its robustness in detecting genetic variants. By obtaining sequence data of multiple genes, this method enables the discovery of novel taxa that may diverge from existing reference sequences but still share enough genetic similarity to allow their “capture” by HCNGS. This capability is critical for uncovering novel or understudied pathogens, such as the potential Ba. microti-like species identified in this study.

A. phagocytophilum is a Gram-negative, obligate intracellular bacterium that primarily infects neutrophils, leading to granulocytic anaplasmosis in humans and various animals 82. It is transmitted by Ixodes tick species, and has a broad host range, including humans, domestic animals, and wildlife. In the United States, I. scapularis and I. pacificus are responsible for its transmission 83. The bacterium’s ability to infect multiple hosts and its widespread distribution makes it a significant concern for both veterinary and public health 82. MLST is a valuable tool for understanding genetic diversity and population structure of pathogenic microorganisms by analyzing sequence variations in multiple housekeeping genes. This is crucial to track pathogen evolution and study transmission dynamics, making it an important tool for epidemiological surveillance and outbreak investigations 46,84.

In this study, we targeted ten genes of A. phagocytophilum, which include the seven housekeeping genes that are part of the MLST scheme for typing this bacterium (pheS, glyA, fumC, mdh, sucA, dnaN, atpA) and three additional genes (msp2-B/msp2-C, msp4，ankA) involved in host-pathogen interactions, that are commonly used to evaluate the phylogenetic relationships of A. phagocytophilum from various species 85–87. Complete or partial sequences of all seven genes targeted by the MLST scheme were obtained from raccoon blood and ear notch samples, with blood samples yielding longer and more complete sequences (Supplementary Table 9). Additionally, sequences from the three other genes (msp4, msp2-C, and msp2-B) were successfully identified, including complete sequences with flanking regions for two genes (Supplementary Table 10). Both the phylogenetic analysis of the five housekeeping genes (dnaN, glyA, mdh, pheS, sucA, Figure 4) and the msp2-B gene (Figure 5) revealed that the A. phagocytophilum sequences obtained in this study cluster with allelic group ST64. This sequence type has been reported in the blood of human cases reported in the US and is considered a key group in understanding zoonotic transmission dynamics of A. phagocytophilum in the U.S46,88. As shown in Figure 4, we could clearly distinguish it from the Ap-V1 strain thought to be non-pathogenic for humans89. The phylogenetic clustering underscores the potential role of raccoons as reservoir hosts and highlights the relevance of this strain in public health. In addition to the targeted regions, we detected flanking sequences for genes like msp2-B and msp2-C, which improved phylogenetic resolution. This highlights the value of capturing extended sequences, and future panel designs should aim to target longer regions to maximize informative content.

Compared to traditional PCR methods, which require designing multiple primers to account for nucleotide polymorphisms, our approach eliminates the need for complex primer development and allows for the detection of diverse gene sequences in a single workflow 46. This efficiency was evident in our ability to generate overlapping sequences for phylogenetic analyses. Furthermore, by targeting the housekeeping genes, which encode proteins essential for maintaining fundamental cellular functions, our method ensures accuracy in detecting A. phagocytophilum 46. At the same time, the inclusion of more specific targets e.g. msp2-B/msp-2C and msp4, which encode polymorphic major outer surface protein involved in erythrocyte invasion, enhances the sensitivity of our approach 85. The genetic diversity of msp2 allows the detection of a broader range of A. phagocytophilum strains, providing reliable coverage across different genetic variants 85,90. These findings not only highlight the strengths of our method in identifying genetic diversity in A. phagocytophilum but also provide valuable insights into its phylogenetic relationships and zoonotic potential. By revealing its association with ST64, our study contributes to a better understanding of the epidemiology of granulocytic anaplasmosis in the United States.

In addition to the primary pathogens targeted in this study (A. phagocytophilum, Bo. burgdorferi, and Ba. microti), our method successfully detected other pathogens, showing the ability for the TICKHUNTER panel to detect multiple pathogens in a single hybridization capture assay. Among the white-footed mice sampled, Bartonella spp. and Rickettsia spp. were detected from mouse ID SM29 while sequencing the blood and ear notch samples, alongside the primary pathogens (Supplementary Table 7). Bartonella spp. are globally distributed bacteria capable of causing a range of diseases in humans, from mild conditions like cat-scratch disease to more severe systemic infections 91. These pathogens are associated with a wide array of mammalian hosts and vectors, reflecting their complex ecology and public health importance 92. Moreover, even though the TICKHUNTER panel did not include a specific reference gene for Coxiella burnetii, three reads from a MiSeq run and 34 reads from an iSeq run were mapped to the C. burnetii 23S ribosomal RNA gene (Table S6, mapping to accession number NR_076084.4). This is likely due to sequence similarity between the C. burnetii 23S rRNA gene and the genes included in the panel for Panrickettsia and Rickettsia rickettsii, which further demonstrates the ability of our method to detect and examine sequences from a broad and potentially unexpected range of pathogens due to its flexibility.

In the curation of the TICKHUNTER panel, we included non-tickborne pathogens that cause similar or “look-alike” diseases, namely febrile illness with no respiratory component. Of these, fleaborne Bartonella spp. are emerging concerns in the US 93. Sequencing of the ssrA gene of Bartonella spp. was successful in 27 out of 39 blood samples from white-footed mice, revealing an overall sequence similarity of 96.9% across a 166 bp region (Supplementary Table 7, Figure 7). Based on these sequencing results, a phylogenetic tree was constructed using a longer region (242 bp) from 17 positive samples, which divided the sequences into two distinct groups. One group clustered with Bar. vinsonii, Bar. grahamii, Bar. japonica, and Bar. coopersplainsensis, with high sequence similarity (99.8%), while the other group was closely related to Bar. rochalimae, with sequences that were identical within the group. Both groups contained sequences from mice sampled in Queens and Staten Island, indicating that these Bartonella species are geographically distributed across multiple urban environments in NYC. The ability to build a phylogenetic tree and observe clear clustering into two distinct groups emphasizes the utility of this method for detecting and characterizing multiple pathogens in sentinel wildlife populations, providing valuable insights into their genetic relationships and prevalence.

The TICKHUNTER panel can be employed in a broad variety of circumstances. The tick samples selected here were relevant to management and/or investigation of reservoirs for viral or unknown etiologies. This molecular resource benefits molecular diagnostics, first by identifying infections with greater certainty than real time PCR and by identifying the host reservoirs of tick-borne pathogens. Our ability to determine tick blood meal sources is an important step in understanding how ticks interact with a wide ecological community over the course of their life cycle. For example, if nymphal ticks predominantly fed on a particular mammal species, we could focus control efforts on that mammal accordingly. Efforts to control tick-borne pathogen transmission have often involved management of wildlife host populations 6,7,94,95. For instance, removal of white-tailed deer to reduce blacklegged tick populations; deer being a primary host for the adult stage of I. scapularis in North America 94–97. However, because deer are not a pathogen reservoir, deer population management sometimes results in an increase in density of infected nymphs (DIN) by diverting immature biting to reservoir competent hosts95,98,99.

Recognition of sequences from the ribosomal 16S rRNA gene present in the mtDNA has been successfully applied for the identification of mammal species 50, including samples in which DNA from multiple species was present and concentrations were as low as 1%70. The use of retrotransposon real-time PCR74 has also been shown to have good results for flat nymphs, although here we were unable to obtain any results with this method on flat adults. This could be due to species coverage and the high specificity of real-time PCR. Our findings support the utility of the 16S rRNA gene for unbiased host identification in DNA extracted from tick blood meals, although further evaluation on flat nymphs is warranted to determine success rates and effects of aging on assay sensitivity. Furthermore, the relationship between the relative proportion of identified host sequences with the life stage at which the blood meal was acquired is yet to be determined. Our results suggest that HCNGS and real-time PCR can potentially complement 16S rRNA metabarcoding for determining tick blood meals depending on the overlap of possible hosts and panel curation (Table 13). One possible approach would be to start with metabarcoding and then follow up with one of the semi-targeted approaches, including HCNGS, depending on the initial results. However, several important limitations should be considered for all bloodmeal identification methods in ticks. High sensitivity molecular detection is prone to contamination, which can introduce false positives or skew host identification in this case. Human results in particular, or sequences from food animals, should be interpreted with caution as they could easily result from environmental contamination. Additionally, the effectiveness of semi-targeted approaches like HCNGS, depends heavily on the host species curation; incomplete panels may lead to misidentification of hosts, particularly when sequences from closely related species are present (as we saw here with the soft ticks) (Table 13). The limited availability of high-quality reference sequences in genetic databases further introduces bias, particularly for non-model or underrepresented wildlife species.

The HCNGS blood meal probes are only useful for environmental vector surveillance, and the authors do not recommend using it on clinical specimens. If clinical samples are run with host probes included on the HCNGS panel, the samples can be dominated by host reads that mask the true diversity of the sample. Environmental samples yield a good balance of host and pathogen reads, even with blood-fed ticks (Table 11). Relatedly, one would need to have coverage of all possible common species for HCNGS to work effectively, otherwise risking inaccurate identification of a closely related species. The retrotransposon real-time PCR should not have this issue as it is designed to be highly host-specific. In scenarios where samples come from completely unknown origins, the 16S rRNA PCR would be the logical choice for balancing species coverage and accuracy.

Furthermore, an important advantage of the TICKHUNTER panel is its applicability to underequipped environments where rapid assessment of tick-borne disease risk is paramount, e.g. economically underserved areas and regions of military deployment. Expanding surveillance efforts will help manage the ongoing tick-borne disease public health crisis. In these cases, the use of the iSeq may be more convenient as it is smaller, lower in maintenance, and much cheaper than the MiSeq platform. Our results show that comparable results were obtained using the iSeq for detecting seven genes useful in the MLST of Bo. burgdorferi. Although not all genes were completely sequenced using the iSeq, it was possible to obtain partial sequences that may still be useful starting information when working in the field.

The dual strategy of combining genes with low variability for accuracy and a highly diverse gene for sensitivity, makes our detection method both precise and comprehensive. In addition, we will incorporate more reference sequences from pathogens that show greater percent identity differences from the currently included reference genes—for example, the Rickettsia rickettsii californica (Rickettsia philipii strain 364D, CP003308.1), which will further strengthen the hybrid capture method’s ability to detect a broader range of pathogens. Beyond ticks and tick-borne diseases, the TICKHUNTER panel could be adapted to fit any disease system of significance because hybrid-capture technology is highly versatile. For example, the host blood detection part of the panel could be used by any researchers working on hematophagous vectors, such as mosquitoes, sand flies, kissing bugs, etc 100–104. Numerous vector-borne pathogens are maintained in zoonotic cycles (e.g., West Nile virus, eastern equine encephalitis, Trypanasoma cruzi, T. brucei, etc.), therefore establishing what hosts vectors are feeding on is a crucial component to understanding pathogen acquisition and transmission. Targeted intervention schemes against specific reservoir species could be implemented following successful discerning of blood meal sources, but this requires good resolution molecular data which hybrid-capture can provide.

sec.title: Data Accessibility and Benefit-Sharing
sec.p: Data Accessibility: All sequences from this study are deposited in NCBI BioProject PRJNA1270965. Metadata were entered into SRA using the MIMARKS.survey.host-associated.6.0 package. All code and associated reference files are available at https://gitlab.com/goodmanlab/. All other data are located in the supplemental Excel file.

Benefits Generated: Benefits from this research accrue from the sharing of our detailed protocol, which is intended to be accessible to any laboratory, and for data and results on public databases as described above.

caption.p: Overview of the hybridization capture method developed to detect, and sequence known tick-borne pathogens (bacteria and parasites), including A. phagocytophilum (in green), Ba. microti (in yellow), Bo. burgdorferi (in blue), and Ehrlichia spp. (in violet).

The linear relationship between the average contig r obtained after hybridization capture and the log of known DNA concentration (copies/uL) for each gene included in the pooled synthetic dsDNA. Each gene targets a different tick-borne pathogen (shown on top of each graph). The average contig r was obtained after hybridization capture and sequencing of five replicates of each dilution series. The coefficient of determination (R2) is shown in the top left corner of each graph.

Maximum likelihood phylogenetic tree of a partial region (2,206 nt) of the 23S rRNA gene of selected Babesia species. The 22 sequences obtained in this study from Queens (Q) and Staten Island (SI) are in bold and form a group closely related to sequences from three Ba. microti reported in humans in New York State in 2022 (blue branches). The name of each sequence includes its accession number, Babesia species, host species, and collection site and year. Numbers in the branches indicate bootstrap percentage values for 1,000 replicates. Branches with <50% support were collapsed. Nucleotide substitution model used: GTR+G+I.

Maximum likelihood phylogenetic tree of five concatenated genes of the multilocus sequence typing scheme of A. phagocytophilum (915 nt). The two sequences obtained in this study are in bold and form a group with sequences from three allelic groups (ST215, ST64, ST161) detected in the USA in cases of granulocytic anaplasmosis from humans and a horse (highlighted in cyan). An additional A. phagocytophilum detected in the USA exclusively in an I. scapularis tick is also shown (Ap-V1, in pink) and does not group with the sequences obtained in this study or those from human cases in the USA (in cyan). The name of each sequence includes its allelic group number (ST), the health information of the host (granulocytic anaplasmosis or healthy carrier), the sample type (blood or tick), the host species, and the country and year of collection. If more than one isolate has been reported for a given ST, the number of isolates is indicated in parentheses. Numbers in the branches indicate bootstrap percentage values for 1,000 replicates. Branches with <50% support were collapsed. Nucleotide substitution model used: K2+G

Maximum likelihood phylogenetic of the partial sequence of the msp2-B gene of A. phagocytophilum (465 nt). The six sequences obtained in this study are in bold and are grouped together with members of the allelic group ST64 (highlighted with a dot). The name of each sequence includes its accession number, the health information of the host if known (granulocytic anaplasmosis or healthy carrier), the sample type if known (blood), the host species, the country and year of collection, and the allelic group if known (ST). Numbers in the branches indicate bootstrap percentage values for 1,000 replicates. Branches with <50% support were collapsed. Nucleotide substitution model used: HKY+G

Maximum likelihood phylogenetic tree of a partial region (481 nt) of the ospC gene of 33 major groups (MG) of Bo. burgdorferi sensu stricto. The 3 sequences obtained in this study from white-footed mice (wfm) are in bold and group within MG A. The name of each sequence includes its MG and accession number. For the three sequences obtained in this study, the host species, collection site and year is also included. Numbers in the branches indicate bootstrap percentage values for 1,000 replicates. Branches with <50% support were collapsed. Nucleotide substitution model used: GTR+G+I.

Maximum likelihood phylogenetic tree of the partial sequence of the ssrA gene of Bartonella (242 nt). The seventeen sequences obtained in this study are in bold and fall into two groups. Both groups include sequences detected in blood samples from white-footed mice (wfm) in Queens (Q) and Staten Island (SI) in NYC. The name of each sequence includes its accession number, the species of Bartonella, strain (str), and isolate (iso) when known. Numbers in the branches indicate bootstrap percentage values for 1,000 replicates. Branches with <50% support were collapsed. Nucleotide substitution model used: K2+G.
Targeted genes or genomic regions used in the TICKHUNTER panel for the detection of 19 tick-borne pathogens.

tr.th: Size of the targeted region (in nucleotides)

td.italic: Borrelia miyamotoi, Borrelia hermsii, B. parkeri, and B. turicatae

tr.td: Junction between ISFtu2 and a flanking 3′ region

fn.p: indicates the genes that were synthesized as synthetic dsDNA and included in the linearity and limit of detection assays.
indicates the genes for multilocus sequence typing (MLST).

caption.p: Information on primers used for real-time PCR analysis

caption.p: Protocols of DNA library preparation and hybridization capture using nucleic acid samples and hybrid capture using DNA libraries and customized panels

tr.td: Step 1DNA fragmentation, end repair, and dA-tailing
Twist Library Preparation EF Kit 1, 2.0 (Frag/AT Buffer and Frag/AT Enzymes)
Step 2Combinatorial dual indexed adapters ligation,DNA purification
Twist Library Preparation EF Kit 1, 2.0 (Ligation Master Mix), Twist CD Index Adapter Set (TruSeq DNA Indexes), Twist Library Preparation Kit 2 (DNA Purification Beads)
Step 3Pre-capture PCR amplification, purification, and quality control

Twist Library Preparation EF Kit 1, 2.0 (Equionx Library Amp Mix (2x), P5/P7 Primer Mix (10x)), Twist Library Preparation Kit 2 (DNA Purification Beads), Qubit dsDNA Broad Range Quantitation Assay (Thermo Fisher Scientific)
Step 2Hybridization of capture probes with pools
Twist Custom Panel, Twist Standard Hyb and Wash Kit v2 with Amp Mix (Hybridization Mix, Hybridization Enhancer), Twist Universal Blockers (Universal Blockers, Blocker Solution)
Step 3Hybridized targets’ binding to streptavidin beads
Twist Standard Wash Buffers v2, Twist Streptavidin Binding
Step 4Post-capture amplification, purification and quality control
Hybridized enriched libraries, ready for sequencing on an Illumina platform
Twist Equinox Library Amp Mix (2x), Twist Amplification Primers, DNA Purification Beads Qubit dsDNA High Sensitivity Quantitation Assay (Thermo Fisher Scientific)

caption.p: Model fitting summary with standard errors (SE) of the linear regression models fitted for each pathogen, presenting the Intercept_SE (uncertainty in estimating the intercept) and Slope_SE (uncertainty in estimating the rate of change).

LOD of eight genes included in the TICKHUNTER panel. Each gene detects a different pathogen, but the eight genes are detected and sequenced simultaneously in each hybridization capture reaction. The lowest DNA concentration detected (in copies/μL) was determined with 95% confidence based on five replicates of 2-fold serial dilutions of pooled synthetic dsDNA.

LOD of Ba. microti in blood and ear-notch samples. The lowest DNA concentration detected (in copies/μL) was determined with 95% confidence based on five replicates of 2-fold serial dilutions of the genomic DNA of Ba. microti added to whole blood and ear-notch samples from laboratory mice.
Sensitivity and specificity of the TICKHUNTER panel.
Two-by-two contingency table for calculating the sensitivity and specificity of Borrelia detection tests in white-footed mice ear notches samples
Two-by-two contingency table for calculating the sensitivity and specificity of Bartonella detection tests in white-footed mice ear notches and blood samples
Result comparison of the detection of Bo. burgdorferi by real-time PCR and HCNGS using the iSeq and MiSeq platforms

Simultaneous hybrid capture detection of host blood meal remnant and tick-borne pathogen sequences from Ixodes scapularis larvae removed from free-ranging Peromyscus leucopus. The average sequencing coverage depths are listed.
Bloodmeal results from questing Powassan virus infected adult I. scapularis tested with untargeted metabarcoding (using conventional PCR of mitochondrial 16S).
Blood meal results from environmental soft ticks tested both on hybrid capture (HC) and host-agnostic metabarcoding (Meta).
